title: Magnetochiral anisotropy in strained superconducting transition metal dichalcogenides
authors: Joaquim Telles de Miranda, Maxim Khodas, Alex Levchenko
abstract: We present a theoretical study of nonreciprocal charge transport in two-dimensional noncentrosymmetric superconductors, focusing on transition-metal dichalcogenide MoS$_2$ as a representative example. In the normal state, the electrical response of a material to the relative orientation of the current and magnetic field is suppressed to leading order in the symmetry-breaking perturbations. In the vicinity of the superconducting transition, magnetochiral anisotropy is strongly enhanced. We consider contributions to the nonreciprocal current originating from order-parameter fluctuations and quantum-interference processes. These terms are linked to higher-order Lifshitz invariants generically allowed in the Ginzburg-Landau free energy of superconductors with broken inversion and time-reversal symmetries. We further show that strain enables additional vector components in the nonlinear current response.
summary: This paper theoretically analyzes magnetochiral anisotropy (MCA) in 2D superconducting TMDs like $	ext{MoS}_2$. It shows that MCA, a nonreciprocal transport effect, is strongly enhanced near the critical temperature due to superconducting fluctuations. The work uses advanced symmetry and fluctuation theories to quantify these effects, highlighting the role of strain in modifying the response.
main_problem: The study investigates nonreciprocal charge transport, specifically magnetochiral anisotropy (MCA), in two-dimensional superconducting materials.
main_result: MCA is strongly enhanced near the superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) due to fluctuation effects, with contributions arising from both order-parameter fluctuations (AL) and quasiparticle interference (MT).
method: The analysis employs theoretical methods combining symmetry group theory, Ginzburg-Landau formalism, and diagrammatic techniques (e.g., Cooperon diagrams) to calculate nonlinear current response tensors.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using superconducting transition metal dichalcogenides ($	ext{MoS}_2$) exhibiting noncentrosymmetric superconductivity in 2D. The theory incorporates symmetry-breaking perturbations like strain and magnetic fields.
key_observables: Nonreciprocal conductivity tensor components ($\gamma_{ijkl}$), the MCA current ($j_{MCA}$), and nonlinear conductivity scaling $\sigma(\mathbf{E})$.
important_parameters: $T_c$, correlation length ($\xi$), disorder scattering time ($	au$), magnetic field strength ($\mathbf{B}$), and strain tensor ($ oldsymbol{\epsilon}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in the diffusive limit ($T_c	au \ll 1$) and relies on separating contributions into AL (collective) and MT (quasiparticle) fluctuation terms.
figures_summary: Not specified by notes, but the structure implies showing derived tensor forms and scaling laws for conductivity components.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from general symmetry considerations of MCA to detailed calculations incorporating both Aslamazov-Larkin (AL) and Maki-Thompson (MT) fluctuation corrections near $T_c$, concluding with an analysis of nonlinear conductivity scaling in the superconducting state.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter superconductivity, the rigorous application of symmetry constraints to derive non-linear transport coefficients ($\gamma_{ijkl}$) is a powerful theoretical tool applicable to understanding directional couplings in complex quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
